EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across New England Thursday afternoon and evening followed by strong northwest winds and cold air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated around 50 kt based on an interpolation of gusts from T.F. Green Airport in Warwick (43 kt) and the 63 mph gust around the same time at Conimicut. Utility poles and wires were down on Hope Street.  In addition, a tree was down on a house and a utility pole was down on Shaws Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the St Lawrence Valley brought strong southwest winds to Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System site at T.F. Green Airport in Warwick reported a wind gust of 53 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across New England Thursday afternoon and evening followed by strong northwest winds and cold air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds brought a tree down on Hartford Avenue in Johnston.  About the same time, winds at T. F. Green Airport in Warwick (Kent County) gusted to 49 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front brought strong southwest winds across Rhode Island during the early and mid morning of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Ocean Service observing site at Newport (NWPR1) measured a sustained wind of 31 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front brought strong southwest winds across Rhode Island during the early and mid morning of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Ocean Service observing site at Conimicut (CPTR1) measured a sustained wind of 33 mph.  The Automated Surface Observation System site at T. F. Green Airport in Warwick measured a sustained wind of 32 mph and a gust of 46 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved off the coast of North Carolina and tracked northeastward, passing south of southern New England.  This brought accumulating snow to areas south of Interstate 90 in Massachusetts, including Connecticut and Rhode Island.  In addition, strong, damaging winds accompanied the snow.  With bare trees, there was remarkably little damage associated with winds that gusted near hurricane force at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately nine inches of snow fell in Bristol County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes and strong high pressure over the Great Lakes resulted in a strong pressure gradient over southern New England.  This produced strong, gusty winds that produced some minor wind damage in parts of Massachusetts and Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed in Richmond on Richmond Townhouse Road.  In Westerly, small trees were downed onto White Rock Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure traveling along a cold front stalled south of southern New England brought heavy rain, which changed over to heavy snow as temperatures dropped. This snow was extraordinarily wet and heavy, bringing down trees and wires across portions of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to eleven inches of snow fell across Bristol County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes bringing heavy rain, thunderstorms, and gusty winds to southern New England. A strong low level jet combined with the heavy rain to result in intermittently strong winds mixing to the surface. These winds resulted in scattered tree and power line damage across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Winston and Long Streets in Warwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved off the coast of North Carolina and tracked northeastward, passing south of southern New England.  This brought accumulating snow to areas south of Interstate 90 in Massachusetts, including Connecticut and Rhode Island.  In addition, strong, damaging winds accompanied the snow.  With bare trees, there was remarkably little damage associated with winds that gusted near hurricane force at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately thirteen inches of snow fell on Block Island.  Snow was difficult to measure due to strong, gusty winds occurring simultaneously resulting in blowing and drifting of snow.  Blizzard conditions may have occurred on Block Island but the observation site on Block Island failed prior to blizzard conditions being recorded.  Winds gusted as high as 76 mph at the Automated Weather Observation System at Block Island State Airport prior to the sensors failing.  Utility poles were downed across the island, knocking power out to most, if not all, the island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful low pressure system tracked up the east coast, passing southeast of Southern New England. This storm brought heavy snow and gusty winds, resulting in blizzard conditions along the Massachusetts east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arnold's Neck Drive in Warwick was closed due to coastal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked north through New York bringing a warm front through southern New England. Unseasonably deep moisture accompanied this front. This was a very complicated weather situation as a strong low level inversion was in place over the area with a very strong low level jet just above the inversion. As showers and thunderstorms developed, the storms and heavy rain allowed the stronger winds to mix down to the surface. In other areas, temperatures warmed enough at the surface to break the inversion and allow the stronger winds to mix down. This resulted in a complicated combination of severe thunderstorm winds and high winds. To add to the historical nature of this event, it occurred in February all during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree in West Greenwich was downed by thunderstorm winds, blocking a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance coupled with cold air aloft and moving into southern New England set off a complicated mix of showers and thunderstorms and non-convective winds. All efforts were made to separate out the non-convective winds from the thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and power lines were downed on Waterfront Drive in Warwick.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 63 mph was reported at Conimicut Light. No significant damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying clipper low pressure system passed north of southern New England, bringing an arctic cold front through the area. This resulted in snow across much of the region and strong, damaging winds near the coast. Unseasonably cold weather then spread across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WeatherFlow reporting site at Point Judith recorded sustained wind speeds of 40 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Matthew was a major hurricane in the Caribbean and Bahamas, then weakened along the Southeast US coast and became post-tropical off North Carolina. The remnants of Matthew merged with a cold front off the coast and moved past Cape Cod during the night of October 9th-10th. This caused wind damage in parts of Eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Weatherflow station URI at Narragansett Rhode Island recorded a sustained south wind of 40 mph at 653 pm EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure approaching from the west brought warm air advection over an anomalously cold air mass at the surface. This resulted in another round of early April snow across much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to eight inches of snow fell across Bristol County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure approaching from the west brought warm air advection over an anomalously cold air mass at the surface. This resulted in another round of early April snow across much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to seven inches of snow fell across western Kent County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow fell across eastern Kent County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall and good mixing combined to bring strong low level winds down to the surface. This resulted in strong to damaging winds across portions of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and branches were downed on Paris Iron Road in Glocester.  In Woonsocket, trees and wires on Talcott Street were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed and intensified along the east coast of the United States, passing well southeast of Nantucket. This resulted in heavy, wet snow overspreading much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of snow fell across Bristol County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed and intensified along the east coast of the United States, passing well southeast of Nantucket. This resulted in heavy, wet snow overspreading much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow fell across eastern Kent County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed and intensified along the east coast of the United States, passing well southeast of Nantucket. This resulted in heavy, wet snow overspreading much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow fell across Newport County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying clipper low pressure system passed north of southern New England, bringing an arctic cold front through the area. This resulted in snow across much of the region and strong, damaging winds near the coast. Unseasonably cold weather then spread across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to seven inches of snow fell across northwestern Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying clipper low pressure system passed north of southern New England, bringing an arctic cold front through the area. This resulted in snow across much of the region and strong, damaging winds near the coast. Unseasonably cold weather then spread across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WeatherFlow reporting site at Halfway Rock recorded sustained wind speeds of 40 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying clipper low pressure system passed north of southern New England, bringing an arctic cold front through the area. This resulted in snow across much of the region and strong, damaging winds near the coast. Unseasonably cold weather then spread across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WeatherFlow reporting site at Block Island Jetty recorded sustained wind speeds of 43 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet combined with rain to produce isolated strong to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Ocean Service Physical Oceanographic Real Time System at Conimicut Light reported sustained winds of 43 mph and gusts to 56 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through southern New England brought showers and thunderstorms to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Walker Lane was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through southern New England brought showers and thunderstorms to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell on Bayview Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwesterly winds mixed to the surface ahead of a strong cold front. This resulted in some damage across southeastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires in Woonsocket.  A tree and wires on Windsor Road were downed in Foster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwesterly winds mixed to the surface ahead of a strong cold front. This resulted in some damage across southeastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree near Hope Road and Burlingame Road was downed in Cranston.  Wires on Blackberry Knoll Way were downed in Johnston.  Several trees and four utility poles on Maple Avenue were downed in North Providence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying clipper low pressure system passed north of southern New England, bringing an arctic cold front through the area. This resulted in snow across much of the region and strong, damaging winds near the coast. Unseasonably cold weather then spread across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration's National Ocean Service Physical Oceanographic Real Time System at Conimicut Light recorded sustained wind speeds of 42 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hermine was named on August 31st as it intensified to a tropical storm in the Gulf of Mexico. She meandered about in the eastern Gulf of Mexico before moving northeastward across the Florida panhandle, through Georgia and then along the coast of North and South Carolina. She was a hurricane as she made landfall in Florida, then weakened to a tropical storm and finally a tropical depression as she moved off the coast of North Carolina. After moving off the coast of North Carolina, Hermine moved northeast, then north, then west, meandering south of southern New England for several days in early September. ||While classified as a tropical system, Hermine's effects on southern New England were rather minimal. Rainfall added up to less than an inch for most locations and winds were generally below tropical storm force. Because trees were still fully leaved and we had higher wind gusts, there was some wind damage. Storm surge was highest during low tide so we had minimal coastal flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WeatherFlow site at Block Island Jetty recorded sustained wind speeds of 41 knots.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Drought Advisory continued for the whole state of Rhode Island during the month of October. The advisory encouraged voluntary water conservation and adherence to any local water restrictions.  ||October brought above normal rainfall to Rhode Island, but continued longer-term drought conditions. For many, the drought began in July 2016. This was a drought categorized largely by well below normal precipitation and groundwater and agricultural impacts. ||Soil moisture (measured by the Climate Prediction Center) was abnormally dry or drier. River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal with 16 gage sites in southern New England at record low levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor placed eastern Kent County in a Severe Drought (D2) designation through the month of October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September brought more dry weather and continued drought conditions to much of southern New England. For many, the drought began in July 2016. At this point, this is a drought categorized largely by well below normal precipitation and groundwater and agricultural impacts. Most of the region experienced below normal rainfall during the month of September. ||A drought advisory was in effect for the whole state of Rhode Island during the month of September. Soil moisture (measured by the Climate Prediction Center) was abnormally dry or drier. Groundwater conditions (measured by the United States Geological Survey) were found to be below to well below normal for most of Massachusetts. River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal with 16 gage sites in southern New England at record low levels. ||Farmers have had to irrigate their crops much more than normal for September. While data thus far has been anecdotal, many farmers are having to choose which fields to irrigate and which to let go, as well as not doing 2nd and 3rd plantings of late summer crops. Providence County was declared a primary disaster county due to the drought. Farms in this county are eligible for assistance from the Farm Service Agency and the Livestock Forage Disaster Program.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued the Severe Drought (D2) designation in northwestern Providence County through the month of September.

Tiverton has no fire hydrants, so firefighters rely on nearby ponds and rivers for their tanker trucks.  With ponds and rivers well below their normal levels, it has become more and more difficult for firefighters to draw water from these sources.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor expanded the Severe Drought (D2) designation into southeastern Providence County on September 13th.  This continued through the month of September.

A tree was downed onto wires on Middlebridge Road.  In Richmond, wires were downed on White Oak Drive.  Trees and wires were downed on Shannock Hill Road.  In Westerly, wires were downed on Wall Street.  In Ashaway, a tree, wires, and a utility pole were downed on Collins Road, near Kenyon Mill Road.  In Hope Valley, a tree was downed on Chepachet Road.

The advisory encouraged voluntary water conservation and adherence to any local water restrictions.  ||October brought above normal rainfall to Rhode Island, but continued longer-term drought conditions. River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal with 16 gage sites in southern New England at record low levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal monthly precipitation that began in March of 2016, continued through August.  In August, rainfall throughout much of Rhode Island was below normal.  ||The governor of Rhode Island issued a drought advisory for the entire state on August 17th.  As part of the advisory, voluntary water conservation and adherence to any local water restrictions were encouraged.  Soil moisture (measured by the Climate Prediction Center) was on the low end of normal while   groundwater conditions (measured by the United States Geological Survey) were found to be normal to below normal for much of the state.  Some privately owned wells were reported to have gone dry in Burrillville.  River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal with 27 gage sites in southern New England at record low levels.||Farmers have had to irrigate their crops much more than normal for August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor expanded the Severe Drought (D2) designation into northwestern Providence County on August 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a warm, humid, unstable environment to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England.  Much of this activity was focused in and around Rhode Island.  Plenty of moisture in the atmosphere resulted in heavy rain with these storms and some flooding was reported in addition to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Route 116 in Scituate was temporarily closed due to flooding.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over northern New England, combined with a strong upper level disturbance sweeping across New England, ushered in colder air and strong to damaging wind gusts across across Massachusetts and Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators reported a peak wind gust to 48 mph at 416 AM EDT.  A tree and wires were reported down in Middletown, RI at 1151 AM EDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on Flat Street in Cumberland.  Not too far to the southeast, a peak wind gust of 42 mph was reported at T.F. Green Airport in Cumberland,  RI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front brought strong southwest winds across Rhode Island during the early and mid morning of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were down on Railroad Avenue in Westerly.  The National Ocean Service observing site at North Kingstown (QPTR1) measured a sustained wind of 37 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed south of New England Friday night the 16th, moved along the Southern New England coast on Saturday the 17th, and moved to the Maritimes by Sunday the 18th. This combined with cold air already in place over the region brought a broad area of snow over Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three and one-half to four and one-half inches of snow fell over Southeast Providence County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between three and four inches of snow fell on Western Kent County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold pool aloft coupled with a large area of instability and a mid level disturbance moving through the area resulted in showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England. The stronger of these storms produced hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Route 116, Station Street, and Knotty Oak Shores Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area tracked across northeast MA late on Nov. 20 and moved northeast of the region on Nov. 21. A potent upper level disturbance moved across southern New England on the 20th.  Gusty southwest winds occurred on the 20th, followed by strong west winds with a few pockets of high winds behind the system on the 21st, causing some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Weatherflow station at the Block Island Jetty (Dunn's Landing) measured sustained wind of 46 mph at 525 PM on the 20th, with gusts to 56 mph.  At the Block Island Airport, the AWOS measured sustained wind of 34 mph at 530 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across New England Thursday afternoon and evening followed by strong northwest winds and cold air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds at Conimicut Point gusted to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was below normal for all of Rhode Island during November, with amounts ranging from near an inch below normal along the south coast to near two inches below normal in the far north and west.||A Drought Advisory continued statewide in Rhode Island during the month of November. The advisory encouraged voluntary water conservation and adherence to any local water restrictions. ||November average streamflows and groundwater levels showed some improvement, except in the southern and eastern regions where they were below normal.  Reservoir levels were reported normal for Newport and above normal for Providence Water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On November 1, the U.S. Drought Monitor reduced the eastern Kent County designation to Moderate Drought (D1) and continued that through the end of the month.
